---
title: "Measurement Invariance"
---

<span class="section-badge advanced">Advanced Topics</span>

::: {.coming-soon}
## 🚧

**This page is under construction.**

Content covering measurement invariance testing — configural, metric, scalar, and strict invariance — will be added here.
:::

---

## What this page will cover

Measurement invariance (also called measurement equivalence) tests whether a scale measures the same construct in the same way across different groups (e.g., cultures, genders, time points). It is a prerequisite for meaningfully comparing latent means or factor structures across groups.

**Levels of invariance**:

| Level | What is constrained | What it allows |
|---|---|---|
| **Configural** | Nothing (same factor structure) | Comparing factor structure qualitatively |
| **Metric** | Factor loadings equal across groups | Comparing relationships (correlations, regressions) |
| **Scalar** | Loadings + intercepts equal | Comparing latent means |
| **Strict** | Loadings + intercepts + residuals equal | Rarely required in practice |

**In practice**: if scalar invariance holds, you can compare questionnaire means across groups. If only metric invariance holds (some intercepts differ = partial invariance), you can compare relationships but not means.

---

## Useful resources in the meantime

- Vandenberg, R. J., & Lance, C. E. (2000). A review and synthesis of the measurement invariance literature. *Organizational Research Methods*, 3(1), 4–70.
- Putnick, D. L., & Bornstein, M. H. (2016). Measurement invariance conventions and reporting. *Developmental Review*, 41, 71–90.

Measurement invariance testing uses CFA — make sure you are comfortable with the [Factor Analysis](factor-analysis.qmd) page first.
